name: Itzamna
title: Supreme Creator God of the Maya Pantheon
symbols: Muan bird (owl associated with death and knowledge)
epithets: God D (scholarly designation in Schellhas system)
The One Who Receives and Possesses the Grace
attributes: Power of creation through word and thought
Mastery of all arts and sciences
Eternal existence as the oldest god

primarySources.title: Diego de Landa's Relacion de las cosas de Yucatan

deities.name: God of Wisdom & Writing | Egyptian Mythology

metadata.historicalPeriod: Classic Maya (c. 250-900 CE) through Postclassic (900-1500 CE)

description: Itzamna (Classic Maya: Itzamnaaj) is the supreme creator deity of the ancient Maya pantheon, embodying the ultimate source of cosmic order, knowledge, and civilization. Depicted as an aged man with a distinctive Roman nose, sunken cheeks, and a single tooth, Itzamna represents the accumulated wisdom of the ages. He created the world, established the calendar, invented writing and hieroglyphic script, taught humanity the arts of medicine and agriculture, and instituted the priestly class. As both the first shaman and the first king, Itzamna legitimated royal authority throughout the Maya world. His multiform nature allowed him to manifest as a cosmic caiman (whose body forms the earth floating on primordial waters), as the Principal Bird Deity (celestial aspect), and as the aged lord of scribes and priests. Paired with his consort Ix Chel, the moon goddess, Itzamna presided over the divine couple whose union ordered the cosmos. His name likely means 'Lizard House' or 'House of the Iguana,' referring to his reptilian aspects and his role as the cosmic dwelling that encompasses all reality. Throughout the Classic and Postclassic periods, Itzamna remained central to Maya religion, invoked in divination, healing, and royal accession rituals, embodying the Maya ideal of wisdom, creativity, and benevolent rulership.

sections.origins: Itzamna's origins lie deep in Preclassic Maya religion, though our clearest evidence comes from Classic period (250-900 CE) inscriptions and iconography. His name appears in the earliest decipherable Maya texts, and his distinctive aged face is among the most commonly depicted deity images in Maya art. The etymology 'Itzam' (iguana/lizard) + 'Na' (house) suggests his role as the cosmic dwelling or the reptilian embodiment of the universe. Colonial-period sources like Diego de Landa's 'Relacion' preserve Postclassic traditions about Itzamna as the first priest and inventor of writing, while the Books of Chilam Balam record prophecies and histories attributed to his wisdom.
sections.attributes: Itzamna possesses supreme divine attributes: he is the eldest of gods, the source of all knowledge, the inventor of civilization's essential arts, and the ultimate legitimator of royal power. His physical form as an aged man emphasizes wisdom and accumulated experience. His reptilian aspects (caiman, iguana) connect him to primordial creation and the earth's structure. As a celestial bird, he represents the sky and solar power. He controls the calendar and cosmic cycles, enabling divination and agricultural timing. His mastery of writing makes him patron of scribes, while his healing knowledge makes him patron of curers. Unlike many supreme gods who become remote, Itzamna remained actively worshipped and invoked throughout Maya history.
sections.significance: Itzamna's significance to Maya civilization cannot be overstated. As the divine source of writing, he sanctified the scribal arts that recorded history, prophecy, and ritual knowledge. As patron of priests, he authorized the ritual specialists who mediated between humans and gods. As cosmic creator, he established the ordered universe within which Maya life unfolded. Maya kings claimed descent from or identification with Itzamna to legitimate their rule. His festivals marked crucial calendar transitions. Even today, elements of Itzamna's worship persist in syncretic Maya spirituality. Comparatively, Itzamna resembles other culture-hero creator deities like Egyptian Thoth, Hindu Brahma, and Aztec Quetzalcoatl, suggesting widespread Mesoamerican and global patterns of associating supreme divinity with wisdom, writing, and civilization's origins.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Maize and Copal
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of maize, the staple food of the Maya, were frequently presented to Itzamna to ensure continued abundance and agricultural success. Copal incense, a sacred resin, was burned to communicate with the deity and seek his blessings for various endeavors.

worshipPractices.name: Ritualistic Writing and Divination
worshipPractices.description: Given Itzamna's association with writing and knowledge, scribes and priests would perform rituals involving the creation and interpretation of glyphs to honor him. Divination practices, aimed at understanding the will of Itzamna, were also common, often using calendars and astronomical observations.

festivals.name: The Celebration of the Mayan Calendar Round
festivals.description: This major festival marked the completion of a full cycle of the Mayan calendar round. Itzamna, as the embodiment of cosmic order and time, was central to the celebrations, which involved elaborate ceremonies, feasting, and the renewal of sacred objects.

festivals.name: Festival of Writing and Knowledge
festivals.description: Dedicated to Itzamna's role as the patron of scribes and knowledge, this festival involved public readings, the creation of new codices, and performances that showcased Mayan intellectual and artistic achievements. Priests and scribes led the ceremonies, seeking Itzamna's continued guidance and inspiration.

cultural.modernLegacy: Itzamna's influence persists in contemporary Mayan communities through oral traditions and artistic expressions that celebrate ancestral wisdom and cosmic understanding. He appears symbolically in neo-Mayan art and literature, representing the enduring quest for knowledge and the preservation of Mayan cultural heritage. His image serves as a reminder of the sophisticated intellectual achievements of the ancient Maya civilization.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: As a creator deity, Itzamna is associated with the life-giving and illuminating power of the sun.

associations.name: Wisdom
associations.significance: Itzamna's aged appearance and role as a teacher directly link him to wisdom and knowledge.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire represents creation, transformation, and the hearth, reflecting Itzamna's role in establishing civilization.

associations.name: Iguana
associations.significance: Some scholars suggest a connection to the iguana, potentially linking Itzamna to earth and primordial creation.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: As the supreme creator, Itzamna represents unity and the singular source of all things.

associations.name: Writing
associations.significance: Itzamna is credited with inventing writing, associating him with communication and knowledge preservation.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White symbolizes purity, knowledge, and the dawn of creation, all aligning with Itzamna's attributes.

companions.name: Ixchel
companions.description: Ixchel is Itzamna's consort and wife. Together, they are considered the parents of the Mayan people and key deities in the Mayan pantheon.

companions.name: Kukulkan
companions.description: Kukulkan, the feathered serpent, is often associated with Itzamna as a bringer of knowledge and civilization. He acts as a facilitator of Itzamna's wisdom to the Mayan people.

companions.name: Bacabs
companions.description: The Bacabs are four aged deities who hold up the four corners of the sky. They are considered servants of Itzamna, helping him maintain cosmic order.

companions.name: Kinich Ahau
companions.description: Kinich Ahau, the sun god, is considered a manifestation or aspect of Itzamna. He protects Itzamna's creations by providing light and warmth.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Divine Lineage
extendedContent.content:
The origins of Itzamna are shrouded in the mists of Maya creation myths, with varying accounts depending on the region and time period. In some narratives, he is self-created, emerging from the primordial void as the embodiment of divine thought. Other accounts depict him as the son of Hunab Ku, the 'One God,' further emphasizing his supreme status within the pantheon. Regardless of the specific origin story, Itzamna's lineage is unequivocally divine, placing him at the apex of the Maya cosmological hierarchy. His emergence marks the transition from chaos to order, from the potential to the actualized world.

His consort is often identified as Ix Chel, the goddess of midwifery, medicine, and weaving, representing the feminine principle to Itzamna's masculine energy. Together, they are seen as the progenitors of the Maya people and the patrons of knowledge and craftsmanship. Their children include deities associated with specific aspects of Maya life, such as the patron gods of scribes and artists, further solidifying Itzamna's role as the source of all creative and intellectual pursuits. The complex relationships within his divine family reflect the intricate interconnectedness of the Maya worldview.

Scholarly interpretations suggest that Itzamna's origins might be rooted in earlier Mesoamerican deities, reflecting a syncretic process where indigenous beliefs merged with those of migrating cultures. However, the specific traits and roles attributed to Itzamna are distinctly Maya, demonstrating the unique intellectual and spiritual achievements of this civilization.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples
extendedContent.content:
While Itzamna was considered the supreme creator deity, direct worship of him was less common than that of more specialized gods. Temples were not specifically dedicated to Itzamna in the same way they were dedicated to Kukulcan or Chac. Instead, his presence was invoked in ceremonies related to creation, divination, and the establishment of royal authority. Rulers often claimed descent from Itzamna, legitimizing their power through divine connection.

The invocation of Itzamna was crucial in calendrical rituals, as he was credited with the invention of the Maya calendar. Priests, considered keepers of knowledge, would perform elaborate ceremonies to ensure the proper functioning of the calendar, maintaining cosmic order and predicting future events. These ceremonies often involved offerings of incense, food, and precious objects, symbolizing gratitude and reverence for Itzamna's gifts.

Evidence for Itzamna's worship can be found in inscriptions and artwork adorning temples and stelae throughout the Maya region. His presence is often implied rather than explicitly stated, reflecting his abstract and transcendent nature. While there may not be dedicated Itzamna temples, the overarching purpose of many Maya temple-cities was to honor the gods and thus also honor the creator god, Itzamna. The reverence shown to knowledge and scribes in Maya society indirectly honors Itzamna, the inventor of writing.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation
extendedContent.content:
Itzamna is typically depicted as an elderly man with distinctive features: a prominent, Roman-like nose, sunken cheeks, and often just one or two remaining teeth. This aged appearance symbolizes his vast knowledge and experience, representing the wisdom accumulated over countless ages. His wrinkled face and stooped posture reflect the weight of the world he created and maintains. While the depiction may seem frail, it signifies the power of the mind over physical strength, emphasizing Itzamna's role as a thinker and creator.

He is often associated with the number four, representing the four cardinal directions and the four corners of the universe. This association reinforces his role as a cosmic architect, responsible for establishing the boundaries and structure of the world. Sometimes he is depicted with iguana-like features, with the iguana being one possible meaning of his name. The iguana represents wisdom and adaptability, further highlighting Itzamna's multifaceted nature.

In hieroglyphic inscriptions, Itzamna's name is represented by a complex glyph that combines several symbolic elements. The precise interpretation of these elements is still debated by scholars, but they generally convey the idea of wisdom, creation, and divine authority. The iconography of Itzamna is consistent across various Maya sites and time periods, indicating the enduring importance of this deity in the Maya worldview.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Itzamna's primary role is to maintain cosmic order and balance within the universe. As the creator deity, he established the fundamental laws of nature and the cyclical patterns of time. He is responsible for ensuring the proper functioning of the calendar, the movements of the stars, and the succession of day and night. Any disruption to this order would be seen as a grave threat to the stability of the world, potentially leading to chaos and destruction.

He represents the power of the mind and the importance of knowledge. By inventing writing and the calendar, Itzamna provided the Maya people with the tools to understand and navigate the world around them. He is the patron of scribes, artists, and intellectuals, inspiring them to create works of beauty and lasting significance. His influence is felt in every aspect of Maya culture, from the intricate designs of their textiles to the complex calculations of their astronomers.

The Popol Vuh mentions that Itzamna was involved in multiple attempts to create humans, indicating the importance of his role in establishing a stable and righteous society. He is not just a distant creator figure but an active participant in the ongoing development of the world. Through his wisdom and guidance, he helps humanity to overcome challenges and achieve its full potential. His legacy is a testament to the power of knowledge and the importance of maintaining harmony between the human world and the natural world.
